turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the court in case 23-867, Republic of Hungary v. Simon.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act or FSIA grants foreign states as well as their agencies and instrumentalities presumptive immunity from suit in the US, but this grant is subject to several enumerated exceptions. Respondents, a group of Hungarian Jewish survivors of the Holocaust and their heirs, sued Hungary and one of its agencies under one such exception, the expropriation exception. This exception permits suits against foreign states when those states take property in violation of international law and the seized property or any property exchange for it has a commercial nexus to the US. Respondents allege that their stolen property has the required nexus to the US because the Hungarian defendants expropriated the property during World War II. Liquidated it, commingled the proceeds with other government funds and decades later, used funds from those commingled accounts in connection with commercial activities in the US. The District Court held that this alleged commingling satisfied the commercial nexus requirement of the expropriation exception and the DC circuit affirmed. The Court of Appeals rejected the notion that the exception requires plaintiffs to put forth allegations that trace the particular fronts from the sale of their specific expropriated property to the US. Because money is fungible, the court reasoned, that the statute permitted reliance on a comingling theory alone and that a tracing requirement would render the exceptional nullity. The court disagrees and therefore vacates the decision below. Allegations of comingling on their own cannot establish the requisite nexus. The plain text of the expropriation exception requires plaintiffs to trace specific property to commercial activity in the US. Either the stolen property itself or property 'exchange for' it. There is no dispute that such tracing is required when stolen tangible property is exchanged for a different tangible object. The statute does not draw lines between tangible and fungible property. Plaintiffs therefore must plead some facts that enable the reasonable tracing of the particular fronts from the sale of expropriated property to the US. A comingling theory alone fails as it distorts the ordinary meaning of the phrase exchange for in the expropriation exception. The theory relies on an attenuated fiction that all the money in a comingled account is property exchange for the stolen property, no matter the size of the account, how much time has passed since the initial comingling or how many intervening transactions in and out of the account may have occurred before the sovereign state use the account in connection with commercial activity in this country. That stretches the plain meaning of exchange for to the point of breaking it. The court's interpretation of the expropriation exception is consistent not only with the FSIA's explicit terms, but also the statute's history. The expropriation exception arose from a specific context, a case where money from Cuba sale of expropriated property was traceable to the US, because Cuba's sale queue segregated and deposited those proceeds into a New York bank account. Congress enacted the expropriation exception against this backdrop permitting claims to proceed when there is a traceable connection between stolen property and the US. Congress also crafted this FSIA to conform fairly closely with international law norms on foreign sovereign immunity allowing a comingling theory without more would expand greatly the circumstances in which foreign sovereigns can be sued in US courts for public acts offsetting the balance Congress struck and potentially inviting retaliatory measures by foreign states against the US. An outcome Congress sought expressly to avoid. Today's decision does not close the door on all cases that involve allegations of comingling. The court holds only that a comingling theory alone is inconsistent with the expropriation exception. Other facts like circumstantial evidence, whether proximity and time between the comingling of funds and a foreign sovereign's expenditure for commercial activity in this country may provide the missing link in a given case. The court recognizes that the moral imperative has been and continues to be to provide some measure of justice to victims of the Holocaust. The government represents that respecting the limits of the FSIA age the US efforts and persuading foreign nations to establish appropriate redress and compensation mechanism for human rights violation. For these and other reasons set forth in the opinion, the court vacates and remands for further proceedings consistent with its opinion. The decision of the court is unanimous.
